author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
text: Aha.....this reminded me of being around 12yrs old in the days when we (in Wales anyway) would always have some serious falls of snow. Fed up of sliding on our shoes one of my mate’s dads bought him a plastic toboggan that went like stink down the local hill (scary speed and quite dangerous really)......anyway, being jealous and not able to afford the super-dooper model I decided to make my own out of wood......longer storey short I had the logic to realise that wooden runners wouldn’t be as efficient as metal and so had the brainwave to cut up a can into a long thin strip and nail it to the runners. Shame I didn’t have the logic to understand just how sharp the edges would be, especially when you’re led on your chest, flying down the hill and can only grab hold of the runners, one to each palm........lots of blood later........funny now but not then......

author: @your_utube
text:
Thanks Robert. A modern twist on the classic quill. Aught to put a damper on the complaints of "nothing in my area". I do think that many people are a bit jealous that they do not have as ready access to second-hand or throw-away sources as you seem to be, but even here in South Africa one has but to look in your own thrash can to see what treasures go to the mounting landfills each day. 
I am quite inspired by the Post Apocalyptic Inventor channel here in YT, who regularly goes to the metal recycling yard in his area (Germany) to dig up many old devices and not-so-old devices that landed there way before their use-by date in order to bring them back to life and back into active service. Great video this.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like him too mate

Anyway thanks again for all your efforts (and injuries for the cause 😉)
Warmest wishes from South Wales 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁷󠁬󠁳󠁿 Jonathan 😊 👍

replies.author: @your_utube

replies.author: @your_utube
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I think you can offer to give them some free marketing. It would be worth it indeed. Years back went to a metal recycling scrap yard to rescue some lawn mowers and they just chuck them on the scale. The unit price per kilogram of metal is way less than the value of the manufactured item. Some scrap yards  do rescue some items in order to sell them instead of recycling them, if they are not too broken. To have people come to them to buy it makes a lot more sense economically because the goods do not need to be all transported to the smelters etc and incur transport costs. How can they refuse?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
That is a very cool project.
With the right attitude, anything can be made from the limited resources found in any area.
Even bedsprings can be used for many projects if you can find away to compress them naturally then you have stored energy. This can be used to turn a flywheel and so many other things. 
We are only limited by the boundaries we ourselves set.

author: @jamescunliffe9872
text: I've made a few tealight candle helicopters using wire and beercans,disc with a smaller disc drawn in the centre with segmenting lines to form lots of rotor blades,cuts stopping at central circle and then in the centre an indentation so it can balance on a rounded off vertical spike so that it can turn freely using the heat from the candle,projects an interesting shadow on the ceiling!:)

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Maybe mix some wax with some carbon from some burnt material (bamboo char?) to fit into the point? Or some chalk?  I like the Gilligan's Island type science stuff. This defiantly could be modified to fit the bill. Just needs a twig to replace the chopstick (cans wash up on beaches all the time).

My neighbor has a couple of old oak trees that grow into my yard. I would have cut the branches back but they are covered in oak galls!  A true gift from God :)
